title: Labneh
subtitle: Strained yoghurt — Lebanon's everyday cheese
excerpt: Plain yoghurt suspended in a muslin cloth and left to drain in the fridge for 24 to 48 hours until it becomes a thick, tangy, spreadable soft cheese. Made from full-fat yoghurt, it is eaten at breakfast drizzled with olive oil, served as part of a mezze, or rolled into balls preserved in olive oil with herbs and dried chilli.
sourceNotes: Labneh (also labne, lebne, or laban) is strained yoghurt eaten throughout the Levant and the broader Arab world. It is documented in Anissa Helou's 'Lebanese Cuisine' (1994) as the most common Lebanese breakfast food. The technique is simply prolonged straining — no cooking, no setting agents, no equipment beyond a cloth and a bowl. Full-fat yoghurt is essential; low-fat produces a weak, watery result.

recipe.batchNotes: Make a large batch — it keeps in the fridge for a week or, rolled in oil, for a month.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Labneh is entirely make-ahead. The longer it strains, the thicker and more concentrated it becomes.

content.text: Labneh is the simplest possible transformation: yoghurt plus time. The whey drains through the cloth, leaving behind the solid proteins and fat. After 24 hours the labneh is still soft and spreadable, like a thick yoghurt. After 48 hours it is firm enough to roll into balls. After 72 hours it approaches the texture of fresh goat's cheese. Stop when it reaches the consistency you want.

Use only full-fat yoghurt — ideally sheep's milk, which has more fat and produces a richer result, but cow's milk yoghurt works well. Salt is added before straining to season the labneh and draw out additional whey. The liquid that drains off (whey) is useful: add it to bread dough, use it as a base for a light soup, or drink it cold. Do not discard it.

Stir the {{sea-salt-fine}} into the {{plain-yoghurt}}. Line a sieve or colander with a double layer of muslin or a clean, fine-weave cloth. Spoon in the yoghurt. Tie the corners of the cloth together and hang over a bowl (or simply set the sieve over a bowl) in the fridge. Leave for at least 24 hours, or up to 48 hours for a firmer result. The whey that collects in the bowl can be kept for other uses.

Transfer the labneh to a plate or shallow bowl. Make a well in the centre and pour over the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}. Sprinkle with {{za-atar}} and {{chilli-flakes}} if using. Serve with warm pitta or flatbread. To store labneh balls: roll heaped teaspoons of the strained labneh into smooth balls, place in a clean jar, cover with olive oil, and add dried herbs, chilli, or lemon zest. Keeps for up to a month in the fridge.

items.symptom: Labneh is still very soft after 24 hours
items.cause: Low-fat yoghurt or cloth not fine enough
items.fix: Use full-fat yoghurt only. If the cloth is too coarse the whey won't strain properly — use a double layer of muslin or a clean linen cloth

items.cause: Yoghurt was already quite sour before straining
items.fix: This concentrates during straining. Counterbalance with good olive oil and za'atar. Adding more salt can also soften the perception of sourness

items.symptom: Labneh is grainy rather than smooth
items.fix: Do not stir or press the yoghurt while it strains — let gravity do the work. Stirring breaks the structure and prevents the whey from separating cleanly

items.symptom: Very little whey has drained after 24 hours
items.cause: Cloth too thick or labneh not cold enough
items.fix: Strain in the fridge, not at room temperature. If using paper towels as a substitute cloth, replace them twice during straining

content.text: Labneh is the Lebanese morning cheese and a fixture on every mezze table. It appears daily at the Lebanese breakfast table alongside olives, pitta, cucumber, tomato, and eggs: the full spread known as a 'mezze breakfast'. In the villages of Lebanon and Syria it has traditionally been made from goat's or sheep's milk and hung to drain in cloth bags tied to beams. The resulting labneh balls, preserved in olive oil with za'atar, could last months and formed part of the winter larder. In its simplest form, a smear of labneh on bread with olive oil, it is one of the purest expressions of Levantine cooking: fermented, bright, fatty, simple.
